pages.text: Technical Note Unconﬁned compressive strength and ductility of ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand Sung-Sik Park * Department of Civil Engineering, Kyungpook National University 1370, Sangyeok-dong, Buk-gu, Daegu 702-701, Republic of Korea article info Article history: Received 5 December 2009 Received in revised form 15 July 2010 Accepted 18 July 2010 Available online 14 August 2010 Keywords: Fiber-reinforced cemented sand Ductility Unconﬁned compressive strength abstract A series of unconﬁned compression tests were performed on specimens of ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand (FRCS) to evaluate how ﬁber inclusion affects the measured strength and ductility characteristics of cemented sand. Lightly cemented sand with three different cement ratios (2, 4, and 6% by weight of soil) was mixed with four different ﬁber ratios (0, 0.3, 0.6, and 1% by weight of soil) and then compacted into a cylindrical specimen. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) ﬁber, which adheres well to cement, was randomly distributed throughout the cemented sand. The test results indicate that the inclusion of PVA ﬁber has a signiﬁcant effect on both the unconﬁned compressive strength (UCS) and the axial strain at peak strength. The increase in the UCS was most apparent in the 2% cemented specimen wherein the UCS increased more than three times as the ﬁber ratio increased up to 1%. The ductile behavior of the FRCS is quantiﬁed by the deformability index, D, which is a ratio of the axial strain at peak strength of ﬁber- reinforced specimen to that of non-ﬁber-reinforced specimen. In the cases of 1% ﬁber ratio, the values of D were greater than four, regardless of cement ratios. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Natural or artiﬁcial cementation of soil particles contributes to settlement reduction and bearing capacity increase [4], which are the two key design considerations in the ﬁeld of geotechnical engi- neering. Owing to such advantages, artiﬁcially cemented soils are increasingly used in various construction sites. For example, Horiu- chi et al. [12] and Kawasaki et al. [15] used cemented ﬂy ash for the construction of a man-made island, and Kitazume [17] also used cemented ﬂy ash for back-ﬁlling waterfront structures, while Is- mail [13] used cemented in situ soil for retaining walls. While the cemented soils markedly increase the elastic modulus and the peak strength, they exhibit more brittle stress–strain behavior at lower initial mean effective stresses or higher cement contents [27]. The brittle failure pattern can cause the sudden failure of soil structures that are stabilized with cemented materials; hence, the usage of cemented soils may not be permitted. In particular, when applying cemented soils at a shallow depth, the degree of brittle failure may be more pronounced due to a low conﬁning stress. To overcome such a brittle nature of cemented soils, either natural or synthetic ﬁbers have been included in cemented soils [7,8,14,20]. The addition of ﬁber to cemented soil produces bond- ing and friction between the soil and the ﬁbers. A ﬁber-reinforced cemented soil can sustain a load even after the debonding or fail- ure of a cemented soil and thus, can effectively improve the brittle behavior of the soil. Polypropylene (PP), polyester (PE), and glass ﬁbers have been commonly used for reinforcing soils because they are readily avail- able [16,25,28,30]. Consoli et al. [6] examined the effect of these three ﬁbers on the mechanical behavior of ﬁber-reinf

2. Unconﬁned compression tests The conﬁning stresses in triaxial testing may destroy bonding or cementation between weakly cemented soil particles [26]. Under such triaxial testing conditions, the soil is already stiff and the ce- ment and/or reinforcement by ﬁbers are not as effective as ob- served under unconﬁned conditions [20]. Hence, a number of researchers [3,11,14,16,18,19,21,23,25,28] have conducted UC tests to isolate various effects on the strength of ﬁber-stabilized sandy or clayey soils. The UC test also determines the strength of cohesive or cemented soils in an inexpensive and practical manner without the need for applying a conﬁning stress, while maintaining soil bonding or cementation prior to shearing. Even though most of the experimental research has used PP and PE ﬁbers, PVA ﬁber, which is suitable for mixing alkaline materials such as cement, is adopted in this study. PVA ﬁbers are generally considered to have a good durability in alkaline environments [24] and superior creep characteristics [9]. The PVA ﬁber is 0.1 mm in diameter and 12 mm in length. It is produced by Kuraray in Japan and its properties are shown in Table 1 . Nakdong river sand is used in the tests and its material properties are summa- rized in Table 2. It consists of silica sand with more than 76% silica and is a sub-rounded sand with a particle size distribution between 2 mm and 0.075 mm. Ordinary Portland cement was used to bond sand particles and ﬁbers. Dry sand and cement with an optimum water content of 14% was mixed and then divided into ﬁve por- tions. Each portion of cemented sand was mixed by hand with a predetermined amount of ﬁber to obtain a uniform ﬁber distribu- tion throughout the entire specimen. Cemented sand mixed with PVA ﬁbers was compacted in ﬁve equal layers and then cured for 7 days. The procedure for the preparation of the specimen is also referred to as Park [23]. The cement ratio, qc, and the ﬁber ratio, qf, are deﬁned as follows: qc ¼ Wc Ws /C2 100ð%Þ qf ¼ Wf Ws /C2 100ð%Þð 1Þ In Eq. (1), Wc is the weight of cement, Ws is the weight of dry soil, and Wf is the weight of ﬁber. The samples were prepared at three cement ratios, viz., 2, 4, and 6%, and four ﬁber ratios, namely, 0, 0.3, 0.6, and 1%. A series of UC tests on 140 mm high /C2 70 mm diameter ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand (FRCS) were carried out at a shearing rate of 1%/min to evaluate its strength and deformability. 3. Results of unconﬁned compression tests 3.1. Strength characteristics Figs. 1–3 show the stress–train behavior of ﬁber-reinforced ce- mented specimens prepared with different cement ratios, namely, 2, 4, and 6%, respectively. Table 3 gives a summary of the measured peak strength (UCS) and the axial strain at peak strength. Fig. 4 indicates that the UCS is proportional to the cement ratio and ﬁber ratio. As the ﬁber ratio increases, the UCS of FRCS increases to more than three times that of the non-ﬁber-reinforced cemented speci- men. From various studies [7,8,20,28] wherein other ﬁbers were added to cemented soils, it is anticipated that the inclusion of PVA ﬁber to cemented sand can increase the UCS. However, the ex- tent of the increase that is due to the inclusion of PVA ﬁber cannot be determined. The increase in the UCS that is due to the inclusion of PVA ﬁber is quantiﬁed by R in Eq. (2). It is the ratio of the UCS of PVA ﬁber-reinforced cemented specimens to that of the non-ﬁber- reinforced specimens. That is, R ¼ UCSðqf ¼0; 0:3; 0:6; 1%Þ UCSðno

1% ﬁber ratio. This could be partially attributed to PVA ﬁber’s adhe- sive properties with regard to cement, which causes more friction between the ﬁbers and the cemented sand. The secant elastic modulus, E50, was calculated from one half of the axial strain at peak strength and was then compared against the cement ratio in Fig. 6 . The secant elastic modulus depends on the cement ratio rather than on the ﬁber ratio. For a given cement ratio, the secant elastic modulus of each specimen, each with a dif- ferent ﬁber ratio, falls within a narrow band of values as shown in Fig. 6, whereas the axial strain associated with peak strength grad- ually increases with increasing ﬁber ratio as shown in Figs. 1–3 . This kind of increasing stress–strain behavior has been similarly observed in the results of triaxial compression tests on cemented specimens with increasing conﬁning stress [1,5]. From these exper- imental results, it can be inferred that in terms of an increase in strength, an increase in the ﬁber ratio in ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand has the same effect as an increase in the conﬁning stress in non-ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand. The horizontal deformation of ﬁber-reinforced samples was restrained by the included ﬁbers. The increase in the conﬁning stress can also restrain the horizontal deformation of the samples. This result has been ascertained by Yang [29] who pointed out that the increase in the maximum prin- cipal stress at failure in the ﬁber-reinforced sample was attribut- able to the increase in the conﬁning stress. 3.2. Deformation characteristics As shown in Figs. 1–3, the axial strain at peak strength increases as the ﬁber ratio increases. This result indicates that the addition of PVA ﬁbers delays failure and improves the brittle behavior. The concept of ductility is introduced herein and used to determine the performance of ﬁber-reinforced cemented soils. For structural materials including FRP (ﬁber-reinforced polymer), the ductility measures the ability of a material to sustain inelastic deformation prior to collapse without a signiﬁcant loss in the resistance [22]. For example, ductility can be deﬁned as the ratio of the ﬁnal defor- mation or deﬂection at the ultimate state to that at the yield state. There are some cases in soil-based inelastic materials, unlike the elastic materials mentioned earlier, where no such clear distinction 02468 1 0 Axial strain (%) 0 300 600 900 1200 1500 Unconfined compressive stress (kPa) C-0(ρf=0%) C-1(ρf=0.3%) C-2(ρf=0.6%) C-3(ρf=1%) Fig. 3. ber-reinforced cemented sand failed at relatively large strain levels of up to 5% compared to PP ﬁber-reinforced cemented specimen that failed at an axial strain of 1.5% [28]. 4. Conclusions Cemented sand was reinforced with randomly distributed PVA ﬁbers for improving the strength and the brittle behavior of the sand. A series of unconﬁned compression tests were carried out on lightly cemented specimens with randomly distributed PVA ﬁ- bers that adhere well to cement. The effect of the ﬁber ratio and the cement ratio on the strength and the ductility of cemented sand were investigated. The following conclusions were drawn. 1. The ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand with 2% cement ratio was a maximum of 3.5 times stronger than non-ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand. The effect of ﬁber inclusion is most apparent in the 2% cemented samples. 2. Owing to the ductile behavior of the ﬁber-reinforced specimens, the axial strain at peak strength of specimens with 6% cement ratio increases up to 5% as the ﬁber ratio increases. 3. A deformability index, D, was proposed for deﬁning the ductil- ity that is induced by ﬁber inclusion in the cemented specimen. D signiﬁcantly increases with increasing ﬁber ratio. At low lev- els of the ﬁber ratio ( qf < 1%), D was not inﬂuenced by the cement ratio. References [1] Abdulla AA, Kiousis PD. Behavior of cemented sands-I testing. Int J Numer Anal Methods Geomech 1997;21:533–47. [2] Al-Refeai T. Behavior of granular soils reinforced with discrete randomly oriented inclusions. Geotext Geomembranes 1991;10:319–33. appears between the yield and ultimate states. Maher and Ho [20] and Consoli et al. [7] deﬁned a stress based index called the brittle- ness index ( IB), which is the ratio of the failure (or peak) deviatoric stress to the ultimate (or steady) state deviatoric stress in triaxial testing, described as a ‘‘brittle component” by Fang and Harrison [10]. However, there is still a difﬁculty in deﬁning an ultimate state (or steady state) for soils, especially for unconﬁned compression tests as shown in Figs. 1–3 . In this study, the term ‘deformability index’ is proposed and de- ﬁned to describe the ductility of FRCS as follows: D ¼ Dfiber Dno fiber ð3Þ In Eq. (3) Dﬁber is the axial strain at peak strength in FRCS, and Dno ﬁber is the axial strain at peak strength in non-ﬁber-reinforced cemented sand. The strain-based index, D, can be determined by comparing the axial strain at peak strength of any ﬁber-reinforced cemented specimen to that of a non-ﬁber-reinforced cemented specimen. Even though most deﬁnitions are usually deﬁned on one curve, the index, D, uses two stress–strain curves, one each from non-ﬁber-reinforced and ﬁber-reinforced cemented speci- mens, as illustrated in Fig. 7. The index is useful when either a peak stress or a residual stress state is not clearly observed. However, its applications are limited to the cemented specimens that have the same cement ratio. The index, D, is calculated and summarized for different cement ratios in Table 3. The value of D increases from 1.7 to 4.5, as the ﬁber ratio increases from 0.3% to 1%. However, the D value seems not to depend on the cement ratio at relatively low ﬁber ratios (i.e., 0.3% and 0.6%). The results obtained by Tang et al. [28] of UC tests on cemented clays with PP ﬁber showed that the value of D for a specimen with 5% cement ratio and 1.5% ﬁber ratio is approximately 3, which is lower than that of 4.3 for the speci- men in this study with 4% cement ratio and 1% ﬁber ratio. This in- dex can be applied to evaluate the ductility or deformability of cemented sand that is reinforced by various ﬁbers. This index may indicate the degree of ﬁber extensibility in cemented sand and aid in the selection of suitable ﬁbers for a given cementation. When the cement ratio is constant, the stress–strain curve tends to decrease gradually following the peak strength as the ﬁber ratio increases. In the case of qc = 6%, the ﬁber ratios of 0.3% and 0.6% may not be enough to satisfy the ductility that is required of the cemented specimen. Fig. 8 a and b describe the axial strain at peak strength for a specimen with the same cement ratio but different ﬁber ratios in 2 D and 3 D plots. It is found that, when the ﬁber ratio is constant, the peak strain at peak strength is almost the same, regardless of the cement ratio. Fig. 9 shows the 1% ﬁber- reinforced cemented sample before and at failure. The failure of the ﬁber-reinforced cemented specimen occurred due to slippage be- tween the sand particles and the ﬁbers. For lightly cemented soils, the use of PVA ﬁber seems to produce a strong frictional interac- tion between the ﬁbers and the soil particles.
